summary:
*The Bronze General. Lord of the First. Warlord of Avernus. Usurper of the Gate. The Shield of Hell. Marshal of the Blooded Host.*

Bel is the archdevil of infernal warfare, military hierarchy, siegecraft, battlefield discipline, martial ambition, brutal merit, and victory purchased through obedience. He rules Avernus, the first Hell, as a warlord enthroned at the frontier of damnation: the commander of burning skies, iron fortresses, blasted killing fields, falling embers, marching devils, siege engines, and roads paved by armies that never stop preparing for the next assault.

He is not war itself. War may be defensive, tragic, liberating, desperate, sacrificial, or forced upon the innocent. Bel is war made infernal: organized violence without mercy, discipline without conscience, courage emptied of compassion, loyalty turned into complicity, and command treated as proof of moral right.

Bel does not tempt by beauty, subtle pleasure, forbidden scholarship, or princely elegance. He tempts through usefulness. He offers the army that arrives before the city falls, the general who can win with broken troops, the weapons no enemy can endure, the formation that survives the impossible charge, the punishment that makes deserters vanish, the promotion that proves worth in blood.

His evil is practical, loud, and hard to romanticize, yet it is not stupid. Bel understands logistics, fear, supply, rank, morale, discipline, fatigue, resentment, and the exact point at which a soldier becomes more afraid of their commander than the enemy. He knows that a battlefield is not won by rage alone. It is won by obedience repeated until the soul forgets how refusal feels.

Bel is the archdevil as general, usurper, drillmaster, quartermaster, siege lord, disciplinarian, battlefield tyrant, and patron of those who believe survival in war excuses whatever is done to secure victory. He does not ask whether violence is justified. He asks whether it worked.
tax:
Bel is a unique archdevil and the sovereign ruler of the first Hell. He belongs among the highest infernal powers as an immortal, lawful evil warlord whose office is inseparable from Avernus, infernal armies, battlefield command, and the militarized defense of Hell’s outermost dominion.

His essential classification rests on archdevil status, infernal sovereignty, lawful evil alignment, rulership of a hellish layer, and command over war as hierarchy, discipline, logistics, conquest, and defensive atrocity.

Bel is not merely a powerful devilish officer. He is a sovereign war power whose authority extends through legions, fortresses, battlefield rites, martial contracts, officer cults, siege-devils, infernal standards, and the formal violence by which Hell protects and enlarges its claims. His rank is not ornamental. It is command made metaphysical.

His nature is strongly martial rather than aristocratic. He is associated with promotion through strength, battlefield utility, practical command, and the politics of military replacement. His title carries the memory of usurpation: not the elegant inheritance of a courtly prince, but the seizure of authority by a commander who proved more useful than the power he displaced.

Bel’s office binds three roles together.

He is gatekeeper, for Avernus is the first field through which invaders, petitioners, raiders, armies, and doomed souls must pass.

He is marshal, for infernal warfare requires order, supply, punishment, training, and endless readiness.

He is usurper, for his own ascent teaches that Hell rewards successful ambition when ambition can be harnessed to hierarchy.

He is most properly understood as the patron of lawful evil warfare as institution. He does not glorify battle because it is glorious. He glorifies battle because it sorts, hardens, consumes, disciplines, and creates chains of command that can survive terror.

Older infernal stems around his name echo the war-god, the storming lord, the devourer of offerings, the idol of command, the bronze-throated master of armies, and the terrible patron invoked when victory matters more than innocence. These strands are not separate identities. They are battlefield masks of the same doctrine: power proves itself by forcing others into formation.
ap:
Bel most often appears as a massive infernal general with bronze-red skin, heavy horns, burning eyes, and a body shaped by centuries of war. He is huge without being bloated, scarred without seeming weakened, and brutal without losing command presence. His face may be broad, severe, tusked, and furnace-lit, with an expression that suggests every person before him has already been assigned a rank, use, and acceptable casualty value.

His armor is usually dark bronze, black iron, or blood-red plate, battered by impossible wars yet never broken. It may be marked by infernal medals, command seals, kill tallies, siege diagrams, and the names of fortresses taken or betrayed. His cloak may be made of flayed banners, smoke, dragonhide, or the torn standards of defeated hosts. His crown, if he wears one, resembles a war helm more than court regalia.

Bel’s weapons are practical symbols of command: greatsword, flaming spear, hooked blade, mace, shield, standard, or rod of battle office. He may carry a map burned into metal, a chain of officer seals, or a horn whose call makes lesser devils stand to attention even when dying.

His manifestations alter the field before the body appears. Distant drums begin beneath the ground. Ash falls like black snow. Armor straps tighten. Weapons grow warm. Cowards taste blood. Officers remember unfinished orders. Campfires lean in one direction, as if toward a marching host. Wounds ache according to rank. The air smells of hot metal, smoke, blood, oil, leather, and trampled dust.

Common signs of his influence include bootprints burned into stone, military orders appearing in ash, standards snapping in windless air, weapons aligning toward a single unseen front, deserters hearing their names called by dead sergeants, and maps marking roads that do not exist until an army needs them.

In monstrous revelation, Bel may appear less like a courtly fiend and more like war given command: wings torn by siege fire, skin plated like heated bronze, horns blackened by artillery smoke, a mouth full of forge-light, and hands strong enough to crush shields into collars. His horror is not chaotic frenzy. It is disciplined slaughter under orders.
eco:
Bel has no natural ecology of breeding, den, or mortal range. His ecology is military. He flourishes wherever fear, discipline, violence, and ambition are organized into permanent command.

His natural habitats are battlefields, fortress camps, siege lines, military roads, armories, war councils, execution yards, barracks, officer academies, conquered cities, infernal mustering grounds, parade fields, weapons foundries, command tents, prison camps, and the rooms where rulers decide how many lives victory may cost.

Avernus, under Bel, is not merely a battlefield. It is Hell’s outer war-machine. Its skies burn with falling fire. Its plains are scarred by ancient campaigns, fresh marches, shattered fortifications, and the wreckage of armies whose names survive only as warnings in infernal drill chants. Roads cut through ash toward citadels, staging grounds, soul-processing camps, and siege works aimed both outward and inward.

The first Hell teaches that war does not end when fighting pauses. The army remains. The supply chain remains. The wounded are sorted. The prisoners are questioned. The dead are counted only if counting serves command. New recruits arrive before the blood dries. Every victory becomes preparation for the next emergency.

Bel spreads by militarization.

One town raises a militia against a genuine threat.
One commander discovers that fear drills faster than trust.
One ruler grants the army temporary authority.
One officer punishes hesitation more harshly than cruelty.
One quartermaster feeds loyal units first.
One chaplain blesses obedience without asking what is commanded.
One generation grows up saluting before it learns to question.
In time, the war may end, but command remains.

His influence favors societies with standing armies, fortified frontiers, warrior castes, harsh military law, expansionist rulers, besieged cities, mercenary companies, veteran cults, conscription, officer privilege, punishment battalions, weapon economies, siege industries, and public reverence for victory divorced from justice.

Yet Bel is not confined to nations or great hosts. He enters villages through emergency militias, noble households through martial tutors, temples through war-priests, companies through brutal captains, expeditions through survival discipline, guilds through armed enforcement, and adventuring bands through the moment when “following orders” becomes easier than deciding what is right.

The most Belian environments are not simply violent. They are places where violence has become orderly, useful, admired, and difficult to oppose without appearing to betray those it protects.
beh:
Bel is direct, severe, ambitious, disciplined, brutal, and grimly pragmatic. He is not without cunning, but he disdains ornament when force, rank, and preparation can accomplish the same end. He values clarity of command, measurable results, competent subordinates, hardened morale, and the ability to continue advancing after ideals fail.

His temptations are addressed to the desperate defender, the ambitious officer, the humiliated veteran, the ruler facing invasion, the mercenary seeking legitimacy, the soldier who survived by obedience, and the commander who has begun to think of mercy as a luxury afforded by people far from the front.

He offers the army that will not break.
He offers the plan that wins by dawn.
He offers the punishment that ends desertion.
He offers the weapon that cracks the enemy gate.
He offers promotion without noble birth.
He offers soldiers who obey without doubt.
He offers supplies when the granaries fail.
He offers victory at a cost that can be counted only after the cheering stops.

Bel rarely disguises the fact that his gifts are violent. His deception lies in making violence feel clean because it is organized. An atrocity performed by a mob horrifies the disciplined mind. The same atrocity performed by uniformed troops under seal can be called necessity, reprisal, pacification, or lawful command.

Toward servants, Bel is demanding but comprehensible. He rewards courage, obedience, battlefield intelligence, endurance, and results. He has little patience for delicate corruption, ornamental decadence, or cleverness that cannot hold a line. A servant who fails through cowardice is despised. A servant who fails through bold action may be reforged. A servant who wins by unauthorized brilliance may be promoted and watched.

He is harsh toward disloyalty, but his definition of loyalty is military rather than sentimental. Personal affection means little. Chain of command means everything. He expects rivals, ambition, and resentment among officers; he punishes these only when they weaken readiness or challenge his authority prematurely.

Toward enemies, Bel prefers overwhelming pressure. He probes, cuts supply, breaks morale, punishes defectors, exploits terrain, captures officers, turns prisoners, and forces foes into choices where every option sacrifices something. He respects worthy resistance only as material to be overcome or recruited.

His central vice is not courage, discipline, or strength. It is the belief that victory is proof of righteousness.
traitsText:
**Tags:** Unique, Deity, Archdevil, Devil, Fiend, Lawful, Evil, Extraplanar, Immortal, Divine, Fire, Earth, Fear, Mental, Compulsion, Contract, Oath, War, Siege, Discipline, Tyranny, Ambition, Fortification, Command, Hell

**Alignment** lawful evil
**Portfolio** war, infernal armies, military hierarchy, siegecraft, battlefield discipline, brutal merit, conquest, fortification, command, martial ambition
**Domains** Evil, Fire, Law, Strength, War
**Subdomains** Devil, Tactics, Siege, Legislation, Leadership, Toil, Tyranny, Ash, Metal, Ferocity
**Favored Weapon** greatsword, spear, mace, or heavy shield
**Symbol** a bronze war helm over crossed blades, a burning standard before a fortress, or a clenched gauntlet holding a broken sword
**Sacred Colors** bronze, black, blood-red, ash-gray, and iron
**Favored Servants** devils, infernal soldiers, generals, mercenary captains, siege engineers, drillmasters, war-priests, execution officers, brutal veterans, ambitious commanders, fortress wardens, and rulers who prize victory above mercy

Bel’s lawful trait is expressed through rank, orders, military codes, formations, punishments, logistics, campaign law, battle standards, officer commissions, chain of command, and obedience under terror. He does not represent battle as wild fury. He represents battle made disciplined enough to become a state of being.

His evil lies in the subordination of personhood to victory. He teaches that soldiers are resources, civilians are terrain, prisoners are leverage, hesitation is treason, and moral doubt is weakness if it interferes with the objective. In his doctrine, the dead are acceptable if the line holds.

Fire belongs to him as artillery, burning pitch, battlefield smoke, siege flame, forged weapons, blasted fortresses, and the red light beneath Avernus’s sky. Earth belongs to him as trench, road, rampart, crater, bunker, fortress foundation, and the ground armies die to claim.

His fear, mental, and compulsion traits arise from command pressure. Bel terrifies as a general terrifies troops who know punishment waits behind them and the enemy before them. He compels through orders, formation, cadence, rank, and the contagious certainty of soldiers moving together toward violence.

Contract and oath are central to his power because armies are made of vows: enlistments, commissions, surrender terms, mercenary contracts, officer oaths, supply covenants, prisoner exchanges, battlefield truces, and blood-sworn banners. A war oath made under Bel’s shadow may outlive the war that birthed it.
abilities:
**Aura of Commanding War (Su)**
Bel radiates an aura of martial discipline and battlefield terror. Lawful evil creatures gain courage, obedience, and tactical focus. Soldiers, officers, mercenaries, and armed servants feel pressure to maintain formation and obey command. Cowardice, hesitation, and mercy under orders become painful to sustain. This is a mind-affecting fear and compulsion effect.

**Lordship of Avernus (Su)**
Bel commands Avernus’s battlefields, fortresses, roads, mustering grounds, siege engines, falling fire, war camps, and infernal legions. In his domain, roads may lengthen to exhaust invaders, fortresses may awaken under attack, ash may preserve footprints of armies, and the sky itself may answer a war horn.

**Infernal Muster (Sp)**
Bel may call, organize, arm, or reinforce devils and mortal soldiers bound by martial compact. Troops gathered under his authority become more disciplined, harder to rout, and more willing to accept cruel orders. The muster does not create loyalty from nothing; it arranges fear, ambition, training, and command into a weapon.

**Contract of Victory (Sp)**
Bel may grant military success, weapons, training, siege engines, disciplined troops, supply, tactical insight, or battlefield endurance through compact. The price is usually framed as necessity: harsh law, conscription, reprisal rights, military autonomy, sacrifice of prisoners, execution of deserters, or permanent recognition of infernal command.

**The Line Must Hold (Su)**
Bel can harden a force against retreat. Soldiers under his influence may continue fighting through terror, injury, exhaustion, hunger, and despair as long as an order remains clear. This endurance is not heroism. It is obedience reinforced until survival and command become indistinguishable.

**Siegecraft of Hell (Su)**
Bel commands the principles of siege, breach, fortification, artillery, countermining, blockade, and assault. He can inspire or direct works of infernal military engineering: towers that climb walls like beasts, rams that remember the gate they were built to break, trenches that drink blood, engines that fire screaming souls, and fortresses designed to punish attackers even after capture.

**Officer’s Brand (Su)**
Bel may mark a servant with infernal rank. The branded officer can command lesser devils, enforce discipline, and draw strength from obedience within their assigned force. The brand also makes failure visible. Officers who break, disobey, or embarrass the chain of command may feel the mark burn like a court-martial written into flesh.

**Tactical Brutality (Su)**
Bel can reveal the most efficient cruel action available in a battle, occupation, or campaign: which bridge to burn, which prisoner to execute, which village to seize, which ally to abandon, which weakness to exploit. The advice is often militarily sound. Its corruption lies in presenting effectiveness as the highest form of truth.

**Punishment Battalion (Su)**
Bel may transform the disgraced, condemned, indebted, or captured into expendable soldiers. Such troops gain a chance at rank, survival, or reduced sentence through violence. The bargain is brutally attractive to those with no other path. It also teaches that worth can be restored only through usefulness in slaughter.

**Burning Standard (Su)**
Bel can invest a banner, horn, shield, or war idol with infernal authority. Forces rallying beneath it become more disciplined and more cruel. Enemies may feel the weight of an army larger than the one before them. If the standard falls, its bearers may be driven to suicidal efforts to recover it.

**Chain of Command (Su)**
Bel’s orders can pass through formal hierarchy with supernatural force. A command issued to an officer may echo through captains, sergeants, soldiers, bound devils, and conscripted auxiliaries. Each subordinate retains will, but refusal becomes harder where oath, fear, training, or ambition already binds them.

**War-Forged Promotion (Su)**
Bel may elevate a servant through battlefield achievement regardless of birth, former station, or prior disgrace. This promotion is real and often intoxicating. It draws ambitious mortals and lesser fiends alike, for Bel’s court recognizes competence when competence produces victory. The promoted servant becomes proof that Hell rewards merit, while concealing what kind of merit it honors.

**Ashen Reprisal (Sp)**
Bel can curse those who break military oath, abandon assigned posts, betray a campaign, violate surrender terms, or flee a lawful infernal command. The curse may manifest as burning footprints, armor that grows heavier with each lie, dreams of the deserted battlefield, or wounds that reopen whenever the deserter hears a horn.

**Hell’s Quartermaster (Su)**
Bel may produce or secure supplies for war: weapons, armor, rations, mounts, ammunition, fortification material, siege parts, medical instruments, and replacement troops. Such aid often arrives exactly when desperation peaks. The cost may be hidden in dependency. An army supplied by Hell eventually fights on Hell’s schedule.

**Conqueror’s Accounting (Su)**
Bel can make casualties, atrocities, and sacrifices appear as necessary entries in the ledger of victory. Commanders under his influence do not forget the dead; they classify them. This classification dulls remorse by converting grief into calculation, and calculation into future doctrine.

**Usurper’s Mandate (Su)**
Bel may sanctify authority seized through successful military action. A commander who takes power by saving an army, winning a battle, or defeating a failed superior may gain a terrifying legitimacy among troops. This mandate does not make the seizure just. It makes it functional, and Hell often treats function as the first step toward law.

**Avernian War Machine (Su)**
Where Bel’s cult or influence dominates, a society can become increasingly organized around military need. Roads straighten for armies, granaries answer to officers, temples bless campaigns, courts defer to security, children learn rank early, and peace is treated as preparation for the next justified violence. The war machine survives by always finding a future front.
bg:
Bel’s legend is the story of a soldier who became a prince by proving that Hell values victory more than pedigree.

He is remembered as a mighty infernal commander, forged in the violence of Avernus and elevated through war, ambition, discipline, and political usefulness. His ascent carries the harsh promise that runs through every army in his service: birth matters less than results if the results strengthen the hierarchy that rewards them.

Avernus shaped him and was shaped by him. It is the first descent, the embattled threshold, the killing ground where Hell receives assault, tribute, invasion, doomed petitioners, and souls beginning their downward passage. Its lord must understand war not as momentary violence but as permanent condition. Bel does. He treats the entire layer as a mobilized organism: fortresses as bones, roads as arteries, legions as muscle, officers as nerves, supply trains as breath.

His usurpation is central to his cultic appeal. Bel is the patron of those who believe command should belong to the one who can hold it. He draws ambitious officers, embittered veterans, low-born commanders, mercenary captains, and disciplined monsters who resent idle nobility. He does not offer equality. He offers promotion through usefulness to a brutal order.

This makes him attractive in times of crisis. When raiders burn the fields, when walls crack, when noble generals fail, when temples speak too slowly, when councils argue while enemies advance, Bel’s servants arrive with orders. They may save the city. They may feed the soldiers. They may rebuild the gate. They may shoot deserters, seize grain, hang suspected collaborators, and make surrender impossible. After victory, the survivors discover which measures were temporary and which have become law.

Bel’s cults are rarely ornamental. They drill. They count weapons. They map roads. They infiltrate officer corps. They advise rulers during emergencies. They create veterans’ societies, punitive orders, mercenary brotherhoods, war colleges, fortress chapels, and secret staff circles where infernal doctrine is taught as military realism.

Their prayers are not always poetic. Many are orders.

Hold the line.
Break the gate.
Feed the host.
Punish the coward.
Promote the useful.
Count the dead later.
Advance.

Bel is defeated not by denying the terror of war or the necessity of defense. Such denial only strengthens his claim. He is defeated by commanders who remember that victory is not justice, soldiers who retain conscience under order, rulers who relinquish emergency powers when danger passes, and communities that honor courage without worshiping command.
note:
Bel is most effective as the patron of militarized necessity: the infernal power behind harsh armies, siege states, emergency command, brutal discipline, martial ambition, veteran resentment, and the belief that survival in war excuses every measure taken to secure it.

His influence may appear through:

a city saved by infernal military discipline that cannot demobilize afterward

a low-born commander elevated by victory and slowly transformed into a tyrant

a mercenary company whose contracts become more binding than civic law

a fortress chapel where deserters are executed as offerings to readiness

a ruler who grants temporary military authority during invasion and never receives it back

a war college whose students learn that mercy is a tactical liability

a punishment battalion that gives condemned prisoners a path to honor through atrocity

a supply officer whose efficient cruelty keeps an army alive

a battlefield standard that inspires courage and extinguishes pity

a veteran order that protects the realm while quietly preparing it for permanent war

Bel’s stories should make danger real. He is weakest when treated as a brute who loves violence for no reason. He is strongest when his methods work. His troops hold. His engines breach. His supplies arrive. His officers make decisions while others hesitate. His victories should create genuine gratitude before they reveal the chain hidden inside discipline.

The moral danger lies in aftermath and habit. Emergency becomes doctrine. Doctrine becomes institution. Institution becomes identity. A community that once fought to survive begins to need enemies in order to remain itself.

Bel’s servants should often be competent, brave, loyal to their units, and contemptuous of soft hypocrisy. They may despise corruption, cowardice, and noble incompetence. Some may save innocents on the battlefield while becoming monsters in occupation. Their tragedy is not lack of discipline. It is discipline severed from mercy.

The question Bel poses is not, “Will you fight?”

It is, “What will you become, if victory is the only virtue left standing?”

And then he orders the advance.
